coredata.dc:title: Carrageenan-derived sulfur, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for sequential detection of Ag+ and Lime Sulfur with “on-off-on” pattern
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Quan
Sun, Lili
Xu, Jiyao
Guo, Xiangjun
Zhang, Hongmei
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doped carbon dots have great potential in sensing field because of their good optical properties, high selectivity and sensitivity to targets. Herein, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (SNCDs) was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal reaction of κ-carrageenan and glutathione. The surface of the prepared SNCDs was modified by active groups, which not only enhance the optical properties, but also improve the analytical detection performance. Then, SNCDs, as a high-efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor, was fabricated for sequence determination of Ag+ and Lime Sulfur (LS). Furthermore, it performed good selectivity and sensitivity to Ag+ and LS even with the interference of other metal ions or pesticides. The linear detection range of Ag+ and LS by SNCDs was 0–100 µM and 0–1 mg/L, and the detection limit was 0.124 µM and 50 µg/L, respectively. More importantly, it has been successfully used in the detection of actual water samples with recovery rates of 98.6~107.1%. In addition, a portable, reusable and highly absorbent fluorescent resin ball was constructed to enrich the application of SNCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Ag+
Lime Sulfur

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Ag+ and LS in water samples
Sensing mechanism of SNCDs to Ag+ and LS
Practical applications of the SNCDs sensor for the detection of Ag+ and LS
Preparation and application of superabsorbent fluorescent resin spheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images, (B) FT-IR spectrum and (C) XPS spectrum of SNCDs. High-resolution XPS data of C1s (D), O1s (E) and S2p (F) of SNCDs.

(A-B) The optical photos of SNCDs with different metal ions under sun light and UV light. (C) FL emission spectra of SNCDs in the presence of different metal ions. (D) The anti-interference performance of SNCDs to Ag+ in the presence of other metal ions. (E) FL emission spectra of SNCDs with different concentrations of Ag+ (0–300 µM). (F) The relationship of (F0-F1)/F0 and Ag+ concentration (0–300 µM). Inset is the linear calibration curve between (F0-F1)/F0 and Ag+ concentration (0–100 µM).

(A) FL response of Ag-SNCDs system with different pesticides. (B) FL intensity of SNCDs, SNCDs +LS, Ag-SNCDs, Ag-SNCDs+LS. Inset: the photos of SNCDs, SNCDs +LS, Ag-SNCDs, Ag-SNCDs+LS (from left to right) under sunlight (top) and 365 nm UV light (down) (C) FL emission spectra of Ag-SNCDs system with different concentrations of LS. (D)The linear relationship of (F2-F1)/F1 and LS concentration. F1＆F2 are the FL intensities of Ag-SNCDs and Ag-SNCDs+LS, respectively.

(A) FL decay traces of SNCDs and Ag-SNCDs. (B-C) Zeta potential change and UV spectra of SNCDs, Ag-SNCDs and Ag-SNCDs+LS. (D-F) TEM, HRTEM and SEAD of Ag-SNCDs. (G-I) TEM, HRTEM and SEAD of Ag-SNCDs+LS.
Synthesis process of SNCDs and description of Ag+ and LS detection.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection.

head.ce:title: Carrageenan-derived sulfur, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for sequential detection of Ag+ and Lime Sulfur with “on-off-on” pattern

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Q. Qi and L. Sun contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doped carbon dots have great potential in sensing field because of their good optical properties, high selectivity and sensitivity to targets. Herein, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (SNCDs) was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal reaction of κ-carrageenan and glutathione. The surface of the prepared SNCDs was modified by active groups, which not only enhance the optical properties, but also improve the analytical detection performance. Then, SNCDs, as a high-efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor, was fabricated for sequence determination of Ag+ and Lime Sulfur (LS). Furthermore, it performed good selectivity and sensitivity to Ag+ and LS even with the interference of other metal ions or pesticides. The linear detection range of Ag+ and LS by SNCDs was 0–100 µM and 0–1 mg/L, and the detection limit was 0.124 µM and 50 µg/L, respectively. More importantly, it has been successfully used in the detection of actual water samples with recovery rates of 98.6~107.1%. In addition, a portable, reusable and highly absorbent fluorescent resin ball was constructed to enrich the application of SNCDs.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, heavy metal ion pollution poses a serious threat to human health and has attracted great attention [1–3]. Silver, as an important heavy metal, has been widely applied in the pharmaceutical, photography, electronic and other fields due to its attractive performance [4,5]. As a result, large amounts of silver are being released from industrial waste into the environment. However, excessive amounts of silver ions can lead to serious harm to human health, such as skin damage, growth retardation, and nervous system damage [6]. Undoubtedly, the monitoring of Ag+ is considered to be significant. In the past few decades, a series of detection methods have been invented to detect Ag+, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [7], stripping voltammetry method [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9] and so on. However, actually, these methods require complicate pretreatment, professional operators and long analysis cycle, which limit the rapid detection of environmental samples and complex food systems [10]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a simple, efficient, and convenient detection method of Ag+.

In recent years, fluorescence method has attracted more and more attention because of its simplicity, rapidity, super sensitivity and good selectivity. Carbon dots (CDs) is zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with a diameter of less than 10 nm [11]. Since its discovery, CDs has occupied a place in the fields of sensing, biological imaging, catalysis, photoelectron and other fields thanks to its excellent performance such as high fluorescence intensity, excellent water dispersion, adjustable surface function and high photostability [12–16]. Many literatures have reported that CDs can detect metal ions, inorganic anions and organic pollutants by fluorescence (FL) quenching or enhanced response [17–19]. Although CDs has great potential in various applications, it needs to be improved by adjusting the core and surface microstructure. Heteroatom doping of CDs has been considered to be an effective method to modulate the inherent properties of CDs. Many attempts have been approved that doping heteroatom in CDs, such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorous (P) and fluorine (F), etc. can not only enhance the fluorescence efficiency, but also provide active sites in the CDs, so as to improve their sensing selectivity and sensitivity towards targets. For example, Chen et al. prepared N, B, F-CDs by one-step hydrothermal method with malonate and 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid for the detection of sulfathiazole with extremely low detection limit [20]. Notably, some methods for the detection of Ag+ with doped CDs have also been reported [21,22]. Jiang et al. used hemicellulose as the precursor to synthesize N-HCDs for the detection of Ag+. The LOD of Ag+ is 21 nM, the detection range is 0–2.5 µM [23]. Wu et al. synthesized nitrogen-rich carbon dots (NCDs) for Ag+ and Hg2+ detection. The LOD and the detection range of Ag+ were 0.48 μM and 0–20 μM [24]. Due to the existence of active groups such as -NH2 on the surface of CDs, it is easy to chelate with Ag+, which leads to CDs aggregation and FL quenching [13,25]. Nonetheless, these N-doped CDs have a relatively small detection range for Ag+, which are not suitable for widespread use. Therefore, it is necessary to change the detection range of CDs to Ag+ by modifying more active groups on the surface, such as -SH, -S(O)- x and so on.

κ-Carrageenan is a natural polysaccharide found in many red algae, and its galactose residues contain large amounts of active groups of the half ester sulfate group (ROSO3 -), which can be modified on CDs during carbonization process. So it is an appealing candidate as precursor for the synthesis CDs [26,27]. The structure of glutathione is rich in N elements, and the main functional group is -SH [28]. So far, there are few CDs synthesized by glutathione. The main objective of the present study was to prepare S, N co-doped CDs (SNCDs) by using κ-carrageenan and glutathione. The prepared SNCDs not only possess excellent optical properties, but also improve the analytical detection performance due to the active groups modified on the surface of SNCDs, and achieved the specific detection of Ag+ and Lime Sulfur (LS). Ag+ not only have a strong affinity with carboxyl (-COO-), sulfate (-OSO3 -) on the surface of SNCDs, but also can be reduced to Ag0 by -SH, -OH and -NH2, then formed silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs). The formed Ag NPs induced the internal filter effect (IFE), leading to the fluorescence quenching of SNCDs. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered by LS due to the stronger reaction of LS with Ag NPs, so that IFE induced by Ag NPs disappear ( Scheme 1). More importantly, the actual samples were detected and analyzed with SNCDs as fluorescent sensor. This method not only verify the efficiency and accuracy of the developed method for target determination, but also prove that the composition in the actual sample does not affect the test results. Furthermore, in order to facilitate the use of SNCDs, effective adsorption materials were selected to adsorb and store SNCDs, and fluorescent superabsorbent resin spheres were successfully prepared.

κ-Carrageenan was purchased from Shishi globe agar industrial Co., Ltd. (Quanzhou, China). Glutathione was purchased by Love Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (shanghai, China), M-phenylene diamine was bought by National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Other metal salts [CaCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, MgCl2, HgCl2, BaCl2, CdCl2, AlCl3, KI, AgNO3, Ni(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2] were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). Pesticides [Lime Sulfur (LS), Boscalid (Bos), Cyfluthrin (Cyf), Kasugamycin (KSM), Carbendazim (Carb), Zinc thiazole (ZTH), Cyromazine (Cyro), Phoxim (Pho), Isoprocorb (Isop), Enidylpyridine (Enid), Procymidone (Proc) and Flonicamid (ARIA)] were brought by Institute of Plant Protection, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. Resin balls were bought in supermarkets. All chemical reagents are commercially available and do not require further purification.

Dissolved 0.25 g glutathione in 10 mL 1% carrageenan solution, ultrasonic agitation to obtain uniform solution. The solution was transferred to a 20 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in a muffle furnace for hydrothermal carbonization at 180 °C for 18 h. Then cooled to room temperature. The obtained yellow solution was transferred to the centrifugal tube, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered. Dialyzed with deionized water in a 1 kDa dialysis bag for 24 h to remove unreacted chemicals and obtain a transparent solution of SNCDs. In the end, solid samples of SNCDs were obtained under vacuum freeze-drying conditions for further characterization and application. An undoped CDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of 10 mL citric acid (0.5 g) under identical conditions.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of SNCDs was implemented with a JEM-1200 EX (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscopy operating at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was presented with a D/MAX-RB X-ray diffractometer, measuring with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 10–80°. FL measurements were measured on a Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). The UV–vis spectra were obtained by a TU-1901 (Persee, Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out on spectrometer (IR00, USA) by KBr tablet method with scanning range 500–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded using Thermofisher DXR2 Raman spectrometer with 532 nm excitation wavelength. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). FL decay curve was recorded by an Edinburgh FLS980 at excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

The selectivity of SNCDs to Ag+ was investigated by adding 15 metal ions respectively, including K+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+ and Ni2+. 10 mM standard concentration stock solution (60 μL) of different metal ions was mixed to 3 mL of SNCDs solutions (0.25 mg/mL). After that FL emission spectra were recorded at 370 nm excitation. Additionally, the anti-interference ability of SNCDs to Ag+ (200 μM) was studied in the presence of other metal ions (200 μM). In order to determine the detection range of Ag+, different concentrations of Ag+ (0–300 μM) were added to 3 mL SNCDs solutions (0.25 mg/mL). Then, FL emission spectra were recorded at 370 nm excitation and FL calibration curves were established.

Similarly, for the detection of LS, Ag+ with a final concentration of 100 μM was added to 3.00 mL of SNCDs solutions (0.20 mg/mL) to obtain a mixture solution, which was named as Ag-SNCD system. Then, different amounts of LS (100 mg/L) were added to Ag-SNCD. The final concentration of LS ranged from 0 to 8 mg/L. FL emission spectra were recorded under excitation at 370 nm. All measurements were repeated three times to ensure the reproducibility of the method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ag+ and LS in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the FL probe based on SNCDs was evaluated by the detection of Ag+ and LS in tap water, lake water and soil. The water sample was filtered through a 0.45 µm filter, and set aside. Soil sample was dried, ground and then passed through a 150-mesh sieve. The 2.5 g sample was then extracted with 50 mL acetonitrile for 0.5 h, and the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was evaporated in a rotary evaporator until nearly dry, and then dissolved in water for later use. The concentration of Ag+ and LS standard solution was 10 mM and 100 mg/L, respectively. During the experiment, the total volume of each sample was kept at 3 mL. The concentrations of Ag+ and LS in samples were determined by standard addition recovery method.

SNCDs solution (0.20 mg/mL) was prepared, and pure resin balls were thrown into SNCDs solution (1 mL each). Then fluorescent resin balls were obtained after 3 h. The fluorescent resin balls were naturally air-dried and stored for later use.

Firstly, the morphology of SNCDs was characterized. TEM in Fig. 1A showed that the SNCDs were spherical and uniformly dispersed. The crystallinity of the SNCDs could be observed in HRTEM image (Fig. 1A illustration) with the crystal plane spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) planar lattice of graphene [29]. According to the particle size distribution diagram (Fig. S1), the average particle size was 2.1 nm. The functional groups on the surface of SNCDs were displayed in Fig. 1B by FT-IR spectrum. The absorption peak at 3437 cm−1 was the characteristic absorption band of O-H/N-H stretching vibration, and the peak at 1648 cm−1 was CO stretching vibration due to the presence of carboxyl group [30]. The peak at 1571 cm−1 belonged to CC of aromatic ring. The peak at 1416 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond. Peaks around 1160–1022 cm−1 were attributed to C-O, C-O-C, C-N, C-S and OSO3 - [31]. The absorption peak at 619 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration of C-S. The existence of these functional groups such as -COOH, -NH2, OSO3 - indicated that the synthesized SNCDs had good water solubility [32]. The compared FTIR spectra for SNCDs and the undoped CDs were presented in Fig. S2. it can be seen that the C-N, C-S and OSO3 - groups do not exist in undoped CDs. X-ray diffraction patterns (Fig. S3) performed a strong peak at 2θ = 20.8°, which is attributed to the (001) plane of graphene [30]. The result was consistent with HRTEM.

Besides, Raman spectrum of SNCDs (Fig. S4A) displayed two typical peaks at ~1600 cm−1 (the crystalline G-band) and ~1398 cm−1 (the disordered D-band), respectively. Where G band corresponds to the amount of graphitization with the sp2-hybridized carbon in SNCDs, whereas D-band is related to defects and functionalization with sp3-hybridized carbon [30,33]. The intensity ratio of G band to D band was found to be 1.58, indicating that the prepared SNCDs have a similar graphitic structure with a certain number of surface defects and surface functional groups [34]. For the undoped CDs, feeble peak of D-band at ~1395 cm−1 demonstrated the mainly graphitic structure with less defects (Fig. S4B).

The elemental composition of SNCDs was characterized by XPS. Fig. 1C was a full-scan XPS spectrum of SNCDs, showing five peaks at 532.64, 400.1, 284.8, 233.1 and 169.6 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s S2p and S2s, respectively. And the contents of the four elements of O, N, C and S are 39.6%, 2.62%, 53.5% and 4.28%, respectively. The C1s peak (Fig. 1D) was divided into three peaks with 284.8, 287.8 and 288.4 eV as the center, which were attributed to CC/C-C, C-N/C-O and CO/CN, respectively [34,35]. CO and C-O-H groups were observed in the O1s peaks of 531.3 eV and 532.0 eV (Fig. 1E), respectively [36,37]. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 1F) showed three peaks at 168.7, 169.2 and 169.8 eV, which belong to sulfur oxide groups(C-S(O)x - (x = 2,3,4)) such as sulfate or sulfonate rather than C-S bond [13]. The results of FT-IR and XPS indicated that N and S elements were successfully doped into the SNCDs, resulting in rich oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur-containing groups on the surface of SNCDs.

Fig. S5 exhibited a series of tests on the optical properties of SNCDs. As can be seen from Fig. S5A, the maximum excitation and emission spectra of SNCDs were 370 nm and 460 nm, respectively. And the emission wavelength of SNCDs had a red shift under the change of excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 430 nm (Fig. S5B). As is generally observed in most CDs, the phenomenon of excitation dependence is related to the functional groups and different sizes of SNCDs [38]. Fig. S5C& D displayed the FL stability of SNCDs under ultraviolet light or different concentrations of salt solution. The FL intensity of SNCDs was almost unchanged, indicating the stability of the prepared SNCDs. Fig. S5E showed the effect of pH (1−14) on FL intensity. The FL intensity ratio F/F0 had a good linear relationship with pH from 5 to 14 (Fig. S5F, R2 = 0.98), indicating that SNCDs is sensitive to pH and can be used to detect the change of pH.

The fluorescent responses of SNCDs in the presence of various metal ions, such as Ag+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+ and Zn2+ were explored. As shown in Fig. 2A, the color of SNCDs became dark brown after the addition of Ag+, while did not change significantly with other metal ions. Meanwhile, the FL of SNCDs with Ag+ quenched under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2B), while others had no obvious change. It could also be seen from Fig. 2C that only Ag+ significantly reduced the FL intensity of SNCDs. Moreover, the quenching response of SNCDs to Ag+ in the presence of other metal ions was similar to that of Ag+ alone (Fig. 2D), indicating good selectivity and anti-interference ability of SNCDs. Then, the sensitivity of SNCDs to Ag+ was further discussed. The FL intensity of SNCDs declined gradually with the increase of Ag+ concentration (0–300 µM) (Fig. 2E). Furthermore, the relationship between FL enhancement factors [(F0-F1)/F0] and the concentration of Ag+ (0–300 µM) was displayed in Fig. 2F, where F0＆F1 is the FL intensity of SNCDs before and after adding Ag+, respectively. And there was a good linear correlation (R2 =0.995, the linear equation is y = 0.005x+0.03) as Ag+ concentration ranging from 0 to 100 µM. After calculation, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.124 µM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Table 1 presented the comparison of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection. The detection range and LOD of SNCDs to Ag+ were comparative or even better than previous reports. These above results demonstrated that SNCDs as an "on-off" fluorescent sensor had good selectivity, anti-interference and sensitivity to Ag+. It is worth noting that this method is relatively simple, does not need complex organic synthesis and complicated instruments, but achieves high sensitivity and better selectivity for Ag+ than other common ions. The developed sensor has prospective applications in environmental and biological detection, and medical analysis.

Based on the FL characteristics of SNCDs and the FL quenching behavior of SNCDs to Ag+, the obtained Ag-SNCDs system (SNCDs 0.20 mg/mL, Ag+ 100 µM) was further used as an "off-on" fluorescent probes for the detection of pesticide. In Fig. 3A, 12 pesticides were selected as the detection targets (1 mg/L), and it was found that only LS could effectively restore the FL of SNCDs. It must be noted that the FL intensity of SNCDs did not change after the LS adding to the SNCDs alone (Fig. 3B). The color and FL changes of SNCDs after the addition of Ag+ and LS could be evidently observed by naked eyes (Fig. 3B inset). The effect of LS concentration on the FL recovery of Ag-SNCDs system was further estimated (Fig. 3C). It can be seen that the FL intensity increased gradually with the increase of LS concentration. Notably, two linear relationships existed in the concentration range of 0–1 mg/L and 1–7 mg/L (Fig. 3D) and the linear equations were y1 = 0.6x-0.012, R1 2 = 0.974 and y2 = 0.07x+ 0.36, R2 2 = 0.973, respectively. And LOD1 was 50 µg/L, LOD2 was 428 µg/L. Thus, this "off-on" fluorescent probe based on Ag-SNCDs has higher selectivity and sensitivity to LS, and can be used to monitor LS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of SNCDs to Ag+ and LS
ce:section.ce:para: The common quenching mechanisms mainly include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoelectron transfer (PET), internal filtering effect (IFE), dynamic quenching and static quenching. Therefore, a series of experiments were carried out to determine the FL quenching mechanism of SNCDs by Ag+. First, the FL decay curves of SNCDs before and after adding Ag+ were presented in Fig. 4A. The average FL lifetimes of SNCDs without and with Ag+ (100 µM) were 4.09 ns and 3.92 ns, respectively. The small change of FL lifetime indicates that the quenching mechanism of SNCDs by Ag+ is dynamic quenching [45]. As shown in Fig. 4B, SNCDs is negatively charged, which have a strong electrostatic attraction for positively charged Ag+ ions. After adding Ag+, the zeta potential of SNCDs increased from − 20.3 to − 12.8 eV. Additionally, FT-IR and XPS spectra proved that there are some functional groups on the surface of SNCDs such as carboxyl (-COO-), sulfate (-OSO3 -), which have a strong affinity for Ag+. Meanwhile, -SH, -OH and -NH2 possess reducing activity, which can reduce Ag+ to Ag0. UV absorption spectrum in Fig. 4C displayed that a new peak appeared at 417 nm after adding Ag+, which corresponds to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption of Ag NPs [46]. The formed Ag NPs was further confirmed by TEM (Fig. 4D). Compared with the TEM image of SNCDs in Fig. 1A, larger particles Ag NPs appeared. Moreover, it could also be observed clearly from the HRTEM (Fig. 4E) that the SNCDs are attached to the Ag NPs. And debyschler rings are clearly displayed in selective area electron diffraction (SEAD) (Fig. 4F), indicating the properties of Ag NPs crystal [6,47]. The newly-appearing absorption peak of Ag NPs at 417 nm obviously overlapped with the emission band of SNCDs (Fig. S6), which illustrates the existence of IFE between SNCDs and Ag NPs [48], and causes the FL quenching of SNCDs.

In addition, the relationship between quenching constant (Ksv) and temperature was also discussed. It was reported that the dynamic fluorescence quenching constant increases, while the static fluorescence quenching constant decreases with the increase of system temperature [48]. The corresponding fluorescence quenching constant can be calculated by the Stern–Volmer equation (F0/F1 = 1 + KSV[C]=1 +Kqτ0[C]), where F0 and F1 are the fluorescence intensity of SNCDs without and with quenching agent, respectively. [C] is the concentration of the quencher (Ag+). KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. Kq is dynamic fluorescence quenching rate constant. τ0 is average fluorescent lifetime of SNCDs in the absence of quencher. The corresponding KSV and Kq value at different temperatures were presented in Table S1. As the temperature increased, the Ksv and Kq of SNCDs solution with Ag+ increased gradually, which further illustrated that the fluorescence quenching process was dynamic quenching. According to Arrhenius formula, the reaction activation energy (Ea) was calculated be about 43 kJ mol−1.

However, after the addition of LS, LS has stronger affinity with Ag NPs and could react with Ag NPs to form silver sulfide, leading to the zeta potential changed from − 12.8 to − 15.9 eV (Fig. 4B) and the SPR absorption band of Ag NPs disappeared (Fig. 4C). Moreover, TEM image in Fig. 4G showed a piece of agglomerate, which is derived from the reaction of LS and Ag NPs. The HRTEM image (Fig. 4H) exhibited the free SNCDs (yellow ring) and the formed agglomerates (red ring). Meanwhile, the debyschler ring vanished (Fig. 4I). These results further confirmed that LS reacted with Ag NPs, resulting in the disappearance of IFE induced by Ag NPs, accompanied with the release of SNCDs and the recovery of the FL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications of the SNCDs sensor for the detection of Ag+ and LS
ce:section.ce:para:
Since SNCDs has excellent specificity and high sensitivity in response to Ag+ (on-off) and LS (off-on), the FL probe was used for the detection of Ag+ and LS in actual samples. The standard addition method was adopted to measure Ag+ and LS in water and soil samples. As shown in
Tables 2 and 3, satisfactory recoveries were achieved. These results indicated that the SNCDs FL sensor is suitable for the analysis of actual samples in the environment with the characteristics of convenient, cheap and sensitive to Ag+ and LS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and application of superabsorbent fluorescent resin spheres
ce:section.ce:para: In order to store SNCDs conveniently, superabsorbent resin spheres were selected as adsorbent. The resin balls were placed in the aqueous solution of SNCDs to make them absorb water and expand, at the same time, SNCDs were adsorbed into the resin balls. The prepared superabsorbent resin spheres displayed strong FL. After drying and dehydrating the fluorescent resin ball, it was found that the resin ball was brown, indicating that SNCDs had been adsorbed and embedded in the resin ball. Moreover, the dried resin balls were placed in water to absorb water and expand repeatedly, and the resulting balls remained fluorescent after absorbing water (Fig. S7). After 6 months of storage, the FL of the dried resin ball hardly changed after absorbing water, indicating the excellent stability of SNCDs in resin ball. More interestingly, the dried resin ball embedded SNCDs can also be a fluorescent sensor for Ag+ monitoring. As shown in Fig. S7, the dried SNCDs resin ball was put into a solution of 100 µM Ag+, and the FL of the expanded SNCDs resin ball becomes weaker. Therefore, SNCDs can be stored in resin balls, which is more stable and convenient than in solution, and can be used as a fluorescent probe for rapid monitoring of Ag+.

In conclusion, SNCDs with strong blue fluorescence were prepared by κ-carrageenan and glutathione. All the characterization results showed that the obtained SNCDs was functionalized by rich functional groups on the surface, which provide the potential to react with analytes, and present good water-soluble dispersion and stable FL intensity. Furthermore, an “on-off” fluorescent sensor was constructed with SNCDs for the quantitative detection of Ag+, and the sensing system displayed wide linear detection range (0–100 µM) and low LOD (0.124 µM) to Ag+, as well as high selectivity and anti-interference. The sensing mechanism of SNCDs to Ag+ based on the principle of IFE induced by the formed Ag NPs and dynamic quenching. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence by Ag+ could be restored by LS due to the interaction between LS and Ag-SNCDs system, thus establishing an “off-on” fluorescent sensor for the determination of LS. More importantly, SNCDs sensing system could realize the practical detection of Ag+ and LS in tap water, lake water and soil samples with satisfactory recoveries. In addition, it was also found that SNCDs can be adsorbed and embedded in resin balls for portable application. This study supplies an effective strategy for the fabrication of multifunctional fluorescent sensor and its potential application in environmental monitoring.

Quan Qi: Experiment, analysis, data collation, writing-first draft. Lili Sun: Experiment, Formal analysis, data sorting, writing-first draft. Jiyao Xu: Sort out data and analyze. Xiangjun Guo: Data sorting and analyze. Hongmei Zhang: Experiment, data collation. Xihui Zhao: Review and revise the article, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41, No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.166129.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
